# Questions in Livestream 13

Live Stream #13: Triskaidekaphobia

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=208" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28</a> | Were the Libyan pharaohs distinct in some way, and what were their reigns like? | Falk described their era as a fragmented Third Intermediate Period in which Libyan rulers imitated Egyptian kingship while retaining some cultural distinctives of their own. | He describes Libyan rule as Third Intermediate Period rule: Egypt was often fractured rather than unified, and Libyan kings tried to reproduce Egyptian kingship while managing Libyan tribal affiliations. Their reigns could therefore look Egyptian in royal presentation while still reflecting Libyan social and political structures. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=412" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">6:52</a> | Where was Caphtor, and is it the same as Keftiu? | He said the majority view treats them as different, with the identification of Caphtor still debated. | Falk says most scholars do not identify Caphtor directly with Keftiu, even though the names and regions invite comparison. He associates Keftiu with Aegean or Greek-island contexts, while Caphtor remains debated and is often discussed in relation to Crete. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=487" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:07</a> | Has he seen the movie *Gods of Egypt*, and is it worse than *Exodus: Gods and Kings*? | He had not seen *Gods of Egypt*, but said *Exodus: Gods and Kings* was bad and that he would watch the other film to compare. | He cannot rank the films because he had not watched *Gods of Egypt* yet. His answer is that *Exodus: Gods and Kings* was already very bad, and the question made him curious enough to go find and compare the other movie later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=535" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:55</a> | What books would he recommend for understanding Revelation through Old Testament symbolism? | He recommended Grant Osborne's Revelation commentary and pointed toward newer Old Testament-in-the-New-Testament style resources. | He specifically recommends Grant Osborne's Revelation commentary, while noting that it may not be cheap. He also points toward resources on Old Testament use in the New Testament and cautions that his own Revelation study was several years old, so newer scholarship may have appeared. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=648" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:48</a> | Will he ever release a solo album? | Maybe someday. He said he does make music, but it remains a time-limited hobby. | He answers playfully that it is possible and says he has done solo music work. The real obstacle is time: music is something he enjoys and would do more of, but producing an album competes with his other work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=720" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:00</a> | If Egypt was weak under Siamun, how could he have campaigned in Palestine and burned Gezer? | Falk questioned parts of that reconstruction and said the issue needs more evidence; a weak king could still mount a limited campaign, but he was not persuaded by the common framing. | He says a weak king could still mount a limited campaign, but he questions whether the evidence proves the usual Siamun-and-Gezer reconstruction. The late 21st Dynasty was fragile and poorly documented, and the biblical text need not mean Siamun personally burned Gezer in the way often assumed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=912" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:12</a> | How can Genesis be intertextual with Egyptian and Mesopotamian myths if they were written in different languages? | He said multilingualism and translation were normal in the ancient Near East, so traditions could cross linguistic boundaries without difficulty. | He explains that ancient scribes and elites often operated across multiple languages, including Egyptian, Akkadian, Canaanite dialects, Hebrew, and Aramaic. Texts and traditions could be translated, summarized, or retold, so language difference does not block intertextual contact. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=1280" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:20</a> | What are the main outstanding problems or inconsistencies in our knowledge of Egypt or the ancient Near East? | He pointed especially to unresolved chronological details and finer political questions, while saying the larger historical framework is usually more stable. | He distinguishes the broad ancient Near Eastern chronological framework, which he sees as generally strong, from fine-grained problems of numbers, synchronisms, and political detail. His example is tension between ancient data and some biblical chronological reconstructions, especially around inflated or difficult numbers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=1629" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:09</a> | Is the suffering servant connected with Job, and when was Job written? | He said the comparison works at the level of archetype, and he would not date Job earlier than the early Iron Age. | He treats the Job comparison as an archetypal link rather than a direct dependence claim. For dating, he would not put Job earlier than the early Iron Age, likely in the period of Israelite kings, and he notes that Mesopotamian influence would not be surprising in the book's setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=1812" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:12</a> | Besides Labib Habachi and Zahi Hawass, does he know other Egyptologists from Egypt? | He said yes, though he did not give a developed list on stream. | He says he knows of others but would need his records for a fuller list. He mentions examples such as James Hoffmeier, who was born in Egypt, and Salima Ikram, while also distinguishing trained Egyptologists from tourism-oriented site officials. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=2014" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:34</a> | Are there first-hand Egyptian accounts of people claiming to witness magic the way the Bible reports miracles? | He said not really; Egyptian texts usually assume magic is real rather than dwelling on eyewitness-style validation. | He says Egyptian culture was saturated with magic, including spells, amulets, execration texts, wax models, and protective rituals. But the surviving record rarely gives first-person eyewitness reports of magic working; it tends to assume magic's reality rather than prove it through testimony. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=2344" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:04</a> | What books would he recommend for Egyptian law, magic, and wisdom, especially for Scripture context? | He recommended starting broadly with *The Context of Scripture* and pointed to specialist works such as Ritner on Egyptian magic. | For broad biblical context he recommends *The Context of Scripture* because it gathers translated ancient Near Eastern texts. For wisdom he points to Egyptian literature anthologies; for law he notes Egypt does not have law codes like Mesopotamia; for magic he especially recommends Robert Ritner and mentions Geraldine Pinch as useful. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=2779" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:19</a> | If there is no direct connection between Satan and Baal, what should we make of Jesus linking Beelzebub and Satan? | He said the connection is indirect: later Jewish demonology could place Beelzebub under Satan, but that is not the same as claiming Baal and Satan were originally the same being. | He reads the Matthew passage as Jesus answering the charge that he casts out demons by Beelzebub. Later Jewish demonological categories could put Beelzebub under Satan, but that does not mean Baal and Satan were originally identical figures. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=3301" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:01</a> | Has he thought about putting Sothic chronology into the Groundhog Lab? | He said no, because Sothic chronology is debated and not something he would treat as a simple settled input. | He says Sothic chronology is too debated to treat as a clean plug-in chronology. Adding it would require choosing sides in active disputes, so he would rather spend development time on clearer additions like Babylonian year names, minor kingdoms, Hellenistic expansion, cleanup, and performance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=3744" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:24</a> | Could "Yahud Melek" on Sheshonq's Bubastite Portal be Judah? | He said it could be related to Judah, but the word order is wrong for "king of Judah"; the damaged inscription may refer instead to a city or place connected with Judah. | He allows a Judah connection but rejects the simple reading "king of Judah" because the word order does not work that way. Because the inscription is damaged, he treats the phrase more cautiously as possibly a city, place, or Judah-related designation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=4062" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:42</a> | Does Genesis 1-5 show more Egyptian influence or more Mesopotamian influence? | He said both influences are present, and it is exceptionally hard to prove that one dominates the other. | He says Genesis 1-5 sits in a world where both Egyptian and Mesopotamian materials could be known and reused. Because the text can resonate with both bodies of tradition, he is reluctant to rank one influence as clearly dominant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=4218" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:18</a> | What is his opinion on reports of tobacco and cocaine traces in Egyptian mummies? | He treated those results as likely modern contamination from sloppy archaeology rather than evidence of ancient Egyptian use. | He regards the cocaine and tobacco mummy claims as almost certainly contamination rather than evidence for transoceanic contact or ancient Egyptian use. His explanation is that older excavations and handling were often messy enough to introduce modern substances. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=4567" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:07</a> | Who was Zerah the Cushite? | Falk thought the figure probably belongs in Egypt's 22nd Dynasty and may correspond to one of the Osorkons, but he did not want to be firmer without more research. | He tentatively places Zerah in the 22nd Dynasty context and suggests an Osorkon as a possible match. He is careful to keep the identification provisional because the evidence is not strong enough for a firm conclusion without more research. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=4956" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:36</a> | What are good undergraduate schools for budding scholars interested in this field? | He said it depends on the student's goals, noted Chicago as strong for Assyriology, and said Wheaton's archaeology strength had already declined because the program was closing. | He says the right school depends on whether the student wants Egyptology, Assyriology, archaeology, biblical studies, or another track. He notes Chicago as strong for Assyriology and says Wheaton's archaeology option had weakened because the program was being closed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=5196" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:36</a> | What should we make of claims that Serapis proves Jesus was made up? | He rejected the comparison as mythicist fringe argumentation and said Jesus' historical existence is overwhelmingly accepted by historians. | He treats Serapis-Jesus comparisons as part of fringe mythicist argumentation, not mainstream historical work. His point is that Jesus' existence is accepted across a wide range of historians, including non-Christian scholars, while Serapis parallels do not overturn that evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=5785" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:25</a> | How is his new plagues book going? | He said he had not had much chance to write because he still needed enough funding to take a sabbatical for the project. | He says progress is limited because the book needs concentrated writing time, and that depends on funding a sabbatical or similar release from other obligations. The project remains intended, but not something he can finish quickly under current constraints. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=5856" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:36</a> | Would Genesis 1-5 have to be written during the exile to have Mesopotamian influence, or could it reflect Amorite influence in Avaris? | He said no exile date is required; stories like Gilgamesh circulated much earlier, and Avaris or Hyksos-Babylonian contact could have been one possible route of cultural interchange. | He says Mesopotamian influence does not require an exilic date because texts and stories like Gilgamesh circulated long before the exile. Avaris, Hyksos-era contact, Amorite movements, and wider scribal exchange could all provide earlier channels for Mesopotamian material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=5995" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:55</a> | Can someone fully learn Egyptian from grammars like Gardiner if they are not studying it at university? | He said grammars can take you a long way, but most people still need at least some help with the alphabet, phonetics, and getting started. | He says a motivated learner can go far with grammars such as Gardiner, but Egyptian is hard to start alone because of signs, transliteration, phonetics, and writing conventions. Some instruction or help at the beginning makes the self-study path much more realistic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=6151" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:31</a> | Could the name Imhotep be equivalent to Joseph? | No. He said the names are neither historically nor etymologically related. | He rejects the identification outright. In his view, Imhotep and Joseph are not linked by name, etymology, or historical setting, so the popular equation should not be used as evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=6235" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:55</a> | What languages does he know, and does he know Arabic? | He said he is not conversant in Arabic, though he picks up some when traveling in Egypt. | He says Arabic is not one of his working conversational languages, though he picks up bits when in Egypt. The answer distinguishes travel familiarity from the kind of language competence he has in ancient-language study. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=6479" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:59</a> | Did Psamtik I have his campaign stopped by the Scythians in the Levant? | He said he did not know enough about Scythian history to answer that confidently. | He declines to give a firm answer because the question depends on Scythian history outside his immediate competence. The useful takeaway is his caution: he will not turn a vague recollection into a confident historical claim. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=6721" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:52:01</a> | What does he think about attempts to move Ahmose to 1570 BCE by redating Khyan? | He rejected that proposal, saying the wider Egyptian and Near Eastern chronology would not allow the shift. | He says moving Ahmose to 1570 BCE by redating Khyan creates too many problems for the broader chronological framework. Egyptian and Near Eastern synchronisms constrain the dates, so the proposed shift is not viable in his view. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=6877" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:37</a> | Can Akkadian be learned from textbooks alone? | In principle yes, but he said it is a brutal language to learn and is much better approached with instruction. | He says Akkadian can technically be self-taught from textbooks, but it is difficult enough that formal instruction is strongly preferable. The writing system, grammar, and reading practice make it a hard language to tackle alone. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=6997" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:37</a> | Is there any attestation supporting the idea that Yahweh was originally an Edomite or Midianite god? | He said a southern or Midianite connection is not absurd, but the evidence is limited and does not prove a simple origin story. | He allows that southern, Edomite, or Midianite associations for Yahweh are not absurd because some biblical and extrabiblical data point southward. But he stresses that the evidence is thin and does not prove a straightforward origin story in which Yahweh simply began as an Edomite or Midianite god. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/orrXTt_zI6I?t=7288" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:28</a> | Does 1 Chronicles 7:20-24 show that Ephraim's tribe was already in the land before the Exodus? | He said no. He read the passage as clan language and genealogical compression, not as proof that the historical Ephraim was living in Canaan before the Exodus. | He reads the passage as compressed genealogy and clan tradition, not a chronological proof that the tribe of Ephraim was already settled in Canaan before the Exodus. The text can describe family lines and remembered events without overturning the broader Exodus chronology. |
